content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      :  Advice Request Meeting Participants                    Date  : May 18, 1983

                                                                   No. M-83-131


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        BOD Leidigm[illegible]

Subject: Recap of Decision Regarding Disclosure/Disqualification By
         Judicial Branch Personnel


        At our May 12, 1983, meeting we discussed several memos and
past Commission policy dealing with disclosure by court
commissioners, retired judges, pro tem judges, and referees. We
also discussed disclosure and disqualification issues with regard
to court administrators, etc., who often do purchasing and make
other governmental decisions which could materially affect
private financial interests.

        We decided that the whole issue of the exemption of the
judicial branch from the provisions of the Act (Sections 82041
and 82049) should be looked at carefully and a legislative
proposal should be drafted for our legislative program next year,
keeping in mind the impact upon the State Bar, Judicial Council,
etc.

State of California                                                          Department of Justice


Memorandum


To    :  Janis McLean, Legal Counsel                              Date  :  May 12, 1983
       Fair Political Practices Commission
       1100 K Street, P.O. Box 807                          File No.:
       Sacramento, CA  95804


From  :  Office of the Attorney General  - Steven H. Kaufmann, Deputy Attorney General
        LOS ANGELES

Subject:  Request for Advice:  Coastal
          Commissioner Roger D. Slates


       Through this office, Coastal Commissioner Roger D.
Slates requests your advice concerning whether he would have any
conflict of interest in voting on matters before the Coastal
Commission involving Chevron USA, Inc. The question arises
because the Coastal Commission, from time to time, is called upon
to review and vote on various oil and gas exploration and
development activities proposed by Chevron USA, Inc., in the
coastal zone and in the Outer Continental Shelf off California.

       To assist you, Commissioner Slates has provided us with
the following factual background. He is a 50% owner and
managing partner of Seacliff Realty Company, in Huntington Beach,
California. Seacliff Realty Company is in the business of
handling real estate matters in the City of Huntington Beach and
Orange County.

       Seacliff Corporation is the other partner of Seacliff
Realty Company. Seacliff Corporation is a wholly owned
subsidiary of the Huntington Beach Company, which is a land
development and holding company. Among other things, it is the
owner of approximately seven to eight oil wells within the City
of Huntington Beach. None of the directors or officers of
Seacliff Corporation sit as directors or officers of the
Huntington Beach Company.

       Chevron USA, Inc., owns 60% of the Huntington Beach
Company. Neither Commissioner Slates, Seacliff Corporation,
Seacliff Realty Company, nor the Huntington Beach Company have
any ownership interest in Chevron USA, Inc. Neither do they
derive any income or direct material financial benefit from any
of Chevron's oil and gas exploration and development activities
in California or in the Outer Continental Shelf off California.

Janis McLean
Page 2
May 12, 1983


Your advice should be forwarded directly to Roger D.
Slates at the address indicated below.

Please let us know if there is anything further we can
do to assist you in your determination.


[signature]
STEVEN H. KAUFMANN
Deputy Attorney General

SHK:gh

cc:  Roger Slates
     1701 Main Street
     Huntington Beach, CA  92648

sections.question: Whether Coastal Commissioner Roger D. Slates would have any conflict of interest in voting on matters before the Coastal Commission involving Chevron USA, Inc., given that he is a 50% owner and managing partner of Seacliff Realty Company, which is partnered with Seacliff Corporation, a wholly owned subsidiary of Huntington Beach Company (which is 60% owned by Chevron USA, Inc.)
sections.question_synthetic: Does Commissioner Roger D. Slates have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act in voting on Coastal Commission matters involving Chevron USA, Inc., where his business partner's parent company is majority-owned by Chevron?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be the incoming request from the Attorney General, not the FPPC's advice response itself. The conclusion/advice from FPPC is not included in this document. The first memo indicates internal FPPC decision-making but is not the response to the Slates inquiry. No explicit CONCLUSION section is present in the Slates request memo.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether Coastal Commissioner Roger D. Slates would have any conflict of interest in voting on matters before the Coastal Commission involving Chevron USA, Inc., given that he is a 50% owner and managing partner of Seacliff Realty Company, which is partnered with Seacliff Corporation, a wholly owned subsidiary of Huntington Beach Company (which is 60% owned by Chevron USA, Inc.)

We also discussed disclosure and disqualification issues with regard to court administrators, etc., who often do purchasing and make other governmental decisions which could materially affect private financial interests. The question arises because the Coastal Commission, from time to time, is called upon to review and vote on various oil and gas exploration and development activities proposed by Chevron USA, Inc., in the coastal zone and in the Outer Continental Shelf off California. To assist you, Commissioner Slates has provided us with the following factual background. He is a 50% owner and managing partner of Seacliff Realty Company, in Huntington Beach, California. Seacliff Realty Company is in the business of handling real estate matters in the City of Huntington Beach and Orange County. Seacliff Corporation is the other partner of Seacliff Realty Company. Seacliff Corporation is a wholly owned subsidiary of the Huntington Beach Company, which is a land development and holding company. Among other things, it is the owner of approximately seven to eight oil wells within the City of Huntington Beach. None of the directors or officers of Seacliff Corporation sit as directors or officers of the Huntington Beach Company. Chevron USA, Inc., owns 60% of the Huntington Beach Company. Neither Commissioner Slates, Seacliff Corporation, Seacliff Realty Company, nor the Huntington Beach Company have any ownership interest in Chevron USA, Inc. Neither do they derive any income or direct material financial benefit from any of Chevron's oil and gas exploration and development activities in California or in the Outer Continental Shelf off California. Janis McLean Page 2 May 12, 1983 Your advice should be forwarded directly to Roger D. Slates at the address indicated below. Please let us know if there is anything further we can do to assist you in your determination. STEVEN H. KAUFMANN Deputy Attorney General SHK:gh cc: Roger Slates 1701 Main Street Huntington Beach, CA 92648
embedding.summary: This document contains two memoranda: the first is an internal FPPC recap from May 18, 1983 regarding a decision to examine judicial branch exemptions from the Political Reform Act; the second is a request from the Attorney General's office on May 12, 1983 asking the FPPC for conflict of interest advice regarding Coastal Commissioner Roger D. Slates and his potential conflicts regarding Chevron USA, Inc. matters.
